 {"id":"br-a2k3h.6","title":"Conformance 6: CI check — new tool requires new fixture (fail fast on drift)","description":"# Conformance task 6: CI drift prevention

## Preconditions

- br-a2k3h.2 + br-a2k3h.3 + br-a2k3h.4 closed

## Scope

Add a CI check (either a dedicated test in the conformance crate, or a CI workflow step) that fails if a new tool lands in TOOL_CLUSTER_MAP without a corresponding fixture.

## Two implementation options

### Option A: test-driven
Add a Rust test that:
1. Loads the authoritative TOOL_CLUSTER_MAP
2. Loads all fixtures (Python-parity + Rust-native)
3. Asserts every tool in the map has at least one fixture case
4. Asserts every fixture-referenced tool exists in the map

```rust
#[test]
fn every_tool_has_a_fixture() {
    let tools = load_tool_cluster_map();
    let fixtures = load_all_fixtures();
    for tool in tools {
        assert!(fixtures.covers(&tool), "Tool {} has no conformance fixture — add one under fixtures/", tool);
    }
}
```

### Option B: CI-script-driven
Add a shell script in `scripts/conformance_coverage_check.sh` that runs the same check and is invoked from `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

Recommended: Option A — keeps the check close to the code and runs on every developer machine.

## Resource coverage

Same check but for resource templates: every template registered in `resources.rs` must have a fixture OR an explicit Rust-native classification.

## Definition of done

- Check committed
- Deliberately adding a new tool without fixture fails CI
- Adding a fixture without a corresponding tool also fails CI (symmetric drift guard)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:25:26.056941173Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T10:46:19.789Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T10:46:19.789Z","close_reason":"Completed: added docs_drift_ci guard for tool and resource drift.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","conformance","drift-prevention"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:25:26.056941173Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:32.939035925Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:33.577686588Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:34.291389069Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a2k3h.7","title":"Conformance 7: fixture regeneration pipeline — auto-refresh Python-parity fixtures on reference update","description":"# Conformance 7: Fixture regeneration automation

## Why this bead exists

The Python reference implementation (mcp_agent_mail Python repo) evolves independently. When upstream changes a tool's output shape — e.g., renames a field, adds a new optional field, changes error formatting — Rust's conformance tests will fail. Each such drift requires:
1. Running upstream Python to regenerate fixtures
2. Diffing old vs new fixtures
3. Reviewing the delta to decide: upstream bug (report it), intentional Python change (match), or Rust divergence (fix Rust)

Today this is manual and slow. Automate it.

## Scope

### `scripts/regen_python_parity_fixtures.sh`

A script that:
1. Clones/pulls the Python reference repo (known upstream URL)
2. Installs/runs the Python reference in a sandbox
3. Drives it with the canonical fixture scenario catalog (list of tool calls + inputs, defined in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-conformance/tests/conformance/fixtures/scenario_catalog.json`)
4. Captures outputs and writes them as fresh fixtures
5. Diffs new fixtures against existing; emits a summary report of shape changes

### CI integration

A **weekly scheduled GitHub Actions workflow** that runs the regeneration and opens a PR with the new fixtures if there are changes. The PR description auto-classifies:
- New fixture (upstream added a tool we don't have): investigate + decide to port
- Shape changed (field renamed): investigate; may be upstream bug or intentional
- Error format changed: usually upstream bug-fix; match
- No changes: close PR / skip

### Classification heuristics

To reduce review burden:
- **Trivial (auto-merge candidate)**: new optional field added, no removal, no type changes
- **Needs review**: field removed, field renamed, type narrowed, error message changed
- **Blocker**: breaking shape change that would make Rust incompatible

## Test plan

- Run the script manually, verify it produces the expected fixture set
- Force an upstream change (e.g., add a debug field), verify the diff report surfaces it
- Run on a scenario with no changes, verify it exits cleanly with "no changes"
- CI workflow tested on a test branch before enabling on main

## Logging requirements

- Every regeneration run produces a log: upstream commit SHA, scenarios exercised, fixtures updated, time taken
- Logs are committed alongside the fixture update PR (not just CI artifacts) — so future blame can trace "when did we refresh against upstream?"

## Non-goals

- Not writing Python changes ourselves (upstream is their own repo)
- Not auto-merging (always require human review for conformance changes)
- Not running Python in CI constantly (weekly is enough; fixture changes are low-frequency)

## Dependencies

- Depends on br-a2k3h.1-6 (audit + classification + CI check) — pipeline integrates with the classification scheme and drift guard established in those beads

## Definition of done

- Script works locally (can produce fixtures from upstream)
- CI workflow scheduled and green on a test run
- First auto-generated PR (if any upstream drift exists today) reviewed and merged or intentionally ignored
- Process documented in conformance crate README","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:49:28.922204317Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T10:58:19Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T10:58:19Z","close_reason":"Completed: Python-parity fixture regen lane landed via automation files in 3159dc9f plus conformance README operator docs in d87fa591","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["automation","conformance","python-parity"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:49:28.922204317Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:51:02.092165791Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:52:00.985491646Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a2k3h.8","title":"Conformance 8: MCP protocol compliance tests beyond tool/resource fixtures (framing, IDs, errors)","description":"# Conformance 8: MCP protocol compliance

## Why this bead exists

The existing conformance harness exercises tool-level and resource-level output shapes against Python-reference fixtures. What it does NOT explicitly test:

- JSON-RPC request/response **ID echoing** (request_id must be echoed by response; MCP spec requirement)
- **Error shape compliance** (MCP errors have specific `code` / `message` / `data` structure)
- **stdio framing** — MCP stdio transport uses newline-delimited JSON; messages must not be split across frames
- **HTTP transport framing** — single request-response per HTTP call; Content-Length or chunked encoding
- **Protocol version negotiation** — client announces supported versions, server responds
- **Initialization handshake** — MCP's `initialize` → `initialized` flow
- **Notifications** — one-way `notifications/*` messages (no ID, no response)
- **Cancellation** — `notifications/cancelled` handling
- **Malformed input handling** — garbage JSON should produce well-formed JSON-RPC errors, not crashes

If `fastmcp-rust` or the MCP spec evolves, our tool-level fixtures won't catch protocol-layer regressions.

## Scope

Add `crates/mcp-agent-mail-conformance/tests/protocol_compliance.rs` with:

### Test 1: JSON-RPC invariants
- Every response echoes request_id exactly (string OR integer preserved)
- Every response has exactly one of `result` or `error` (never both, never neither)
- Notifications (no `id`) never receive a response

### Test 2: Error shape
- Every error has numeric `code`, string `message`, optional `data`
- Standard JSON-RPC codes respected (-32700 parse error, -32600 invalid request, -32601 method not found, -32602 invalid params, -32603 internal error)
- Method-specific error codes documented + used consistently

### Test 3: Transport framing
- stdio: messages delimited by LF; no CRLF unless explicitly escaped; no mixed framing
- HTTP: Content-Type is `application/json`; single message per request
- Both transports: Unicode in strings preserved byte-exact (UTF-8 roundtrip)

### Test 4: Protocol lifecycle
- `initialize` → `initialized` handshake succeeds
- Protocol version negotiation: client requests latest; server responds with compatible
- Unknown tools/resources return method-not-found error, not crash

### Test 5: Malformed input
- Garbage JSON → parse error with code -32700
- Missing method field → invalid request with code -32600
- Wrong param types → invalid params with code -32602
- Server DOES NOT crash on any of these

### Test 6: Large payloads
- Request bodies near size limits (1MB, 10MB) handled or rejected with clear error
- Streaming responses (if any) don't truncate on limit boundary

### Test 7: Concurrent requests (stdio)
- Multiple requests interleaved; responses match correct request_ids (not serialized to request order)

## Test plan

- Each test parameterized across both stdio and HTTP transports
- Reference upstream `fastmcp-rust` test fixtures where applicable
- Include a version-drift test: pin fastmcp-rust to current version; if upstream bumps major, this test fires

## Logging requirements

```
conformance.protocol.test_start { scenario, transport }
conformance.protocol.invariant_check { scenario, invariant_name, pass, detail }
conformance.protocol.test_complete { scenario, transport, passed, duration }
```

Each failure captures:
- Full request payload
- Full response payload  
- Invariant that failed
- Expected vs actual

## Non-goals

- Not reimplementing the MCP spec (defer to upstream)
- Not implementing optional MCP features (e.g., sampling, prompts) unless the project uses them
- Not testing against multiple MCP implementations (python-reference is our comparison, not third parties)

## Dependencies

- Depends on br-a2k3h.1 (audit) — audit should surface whether these gaps exist today
- Coordinates with br-a2k3h.6 (CI drift-guard) — protocol checks run in CI alongside tool/resource checks

## Definition of done

- Test file + 7 scenarios implemented
- Tests run on both stdio and HTTP
- CI-integrated; fails on regression
- One intentionally-introduced regression test (e.g., a shim that breaks ID echo) verifies the tests actually catch breakage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TopazSummit","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:52:54.026711445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T18:32:31Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T18:32:31Z","close_reason":"Completed: added MCP protocol compliance transport coverage and passing conformance tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","jsonrpc","mcp-protocol","transport"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:52:54.026711445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:53:57.119192194Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:53:57.379867858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-a3g6f","title":"Archive commit batching: coalesce mail events into grouped commits","description":"## Why

Today every mail event (send_message, register_agent, ensure_product,
ensure_project, file_reservation state change, etc.) cuts its own git
commit via `git_add_and_commit` / `commit_paths_with_retry`. On a busy
multi-agent mailbox this produces hundreds of commits per minute — each
one a fresh tree and set of loose objects.

The gc tuning from bead (A) and the maintenance task from bead (E) bound
the *cleanup*, but the commit-rate pressure remains. Batching commits
would reduce loose-object churn 10-100x.

## What

A commit-coalescer service:

- Accept commit requests `{ repo_root, paths, message, author }` via an
  unbounded channel from `write_agent_profile`, `write_message`, etc.
- Batch by `(repo_root, flush-window)` with two triggers:
    - N events since last flush (default 32)
    - T milliseconds since first buffered event (default 1000)
  (both overridable via env: `AM_ARCHIVE_BATCH_EVENTS`,
  `AM_ARCHIVE_BATCH_MS`.)
- On flush, call the existing `commit_paths_with_retry` once with all
  accumulated paths and a grouped message like
  `batch: 17 events (2026-04-19T22:31:15Z – 22:31:16Z)`.
- Graceful shutdown: on SIGTERM, drain the queue (flush with timeout) so
  no events are lost.

## Design notes

- Keep the queue *per-storage-root*. In practice a single server process
  only touches one storage_root, but the abstraction lets multi-tenant
  builds scale.
- Preserve the guarantees the current per-event commit gives: ensure the
  archive is always recoverable (no half-written state).
- Observability: emit histogram of batch sizes so we can verify the
  batching is actually coalescing.

## Out of scope

- Replacing git as the archive format (separate discussion).

## Touchpoints

- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-storage/src/lib.rs` — funnel the existing
  `commit_paths_with_retry` callers through the coalescer.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-storage/src/commit_coalescer.rs` — new module.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/config.rs` — two env var readers.","notes":"NavySummit: storage coalescer now initializes its global flush interval from Config::coalescer_flush_ms instead of hardcoded DEFAULT_COALESCER_FLUSH_MS; added focused env override unit coverage. Verified storage package fmt/check/test/clippy via rch. Core config alias work for AM_ARCHIVE_BATCH_* remains blocked by GrayRidge's active config.rs reservation.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-19T23:58:43.843409025Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:50:49.176200460Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["archive","performance","scalability"]}

 {"id":"br-aazao","title":"Verification Coverage Program: real-path unit tests, E2E completeness, and forensic logging","description":"# Verification Coverage Program: Real-Path Unit Tests, E2E Completeness, and Forensic Logging

## Why
The repository already has a large amount of testing surface area, but it would be inaccurate to claim any of the following today:
- full unit coverage across the workspace
- zero reliance on mocks, stubs, fake binaries, fake repos, or synthetic fixtures
- complete end-to-end coverage across every critical workflow and failure mode
- uniformly excellent, information-dense logging and artifact capture across all E2E suites

Current evidence for those gaps is already visible in-tree:
- deterministic stub encoder coverage in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/tests/toon_integration.rs`
- stub embedder / stub engine coverage in search-core and DB search-bridge tests
- stubbed LLM completions in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-tools/src/llm.rs`
- mock release artifacts in `tests/e2e/test_idempotency.sh`
- fake repo / fake lock / synthetic owner-state scenarios in storage tests
- wrapper-style E2E entrypoints where artifact richness and logging density are not guaranteed to be identical suite-to-suite

## Goal
Create a disciplined verification program that:
- maps every important module and workflow to concrete tests
- maximizes real-path coverage rather than simulated-only confidence
- explicitly documents any remaining sanctioned substitutes that cannot yet be removed
- standardizes E2E artifact capture, logging density, and rerun breadcrumbs
- installs CI gates so future contributors cannot quietly regress realism or coverage

## Deliverables
- repo-wide audit of test coverage, realism, and logging quality
- policy for what counts as acceptable test realism and when substitutes are allowed
- burn-down plan for fake-only critical-path tests
- granular unit-coverage closure across crate clusters
- granular E2E matrix closure across transport, workflow, failure, and performance paths
- forensic artifact and logging contract for all E2E suites
- CI/reporting/sign-off layer for maintaining the standard

## Acceptance Criteria
- a future maintainer can answer, with evidence rather than vibes, which critical paths are covered by real-path unit tests, which are covered by E2E, which still rely on sanctioned substitutes, and why
- no critical user-visible or operator-visible path is validated only by ad hoc stubs or thin happy-path E2E wrappers
- failures produce enough structured data to debug without blind reruns
- the resulting bead tree is self-contained enough that implementation can proceed without needing to reconstruct this reasoning from chat history

## Important Notes
This is a repo-wide verification hardening program, not a single test bead. The point is to turn broad but uneven coverage into trustworthy and explainable coverage.","status":"tombstone","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":0,"created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.708072106Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035073367Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035073367Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035014998Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"epic","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-aazao.1","title":"Verification Coverage 1.1: repo-wide audit of test realism, gaps, and ownership","description":"# Verification Coverage 1.1: Repo-Wide Audit of Test Realism, Gaps, and Ownership

## Why
Before adding more tests, the project needs an explicit inventory of what is already covered, how that coverage is achieved, and where confidence is artificially inflated by substitutes, wrappers, or fixture-only paths.

## Deliverables
- enumerate the workspace by crate, major module cluster, and critical workflow surface
- map each surface to existing unit, property, integration, conformance, and E2E coverage
- classify each test by realism grade:
  - real-path / real dependency
  - deterministic local fixture
  - sanctioned substitute
  - mock / fake / stub
  - untested
- identify which tests are merely contract or formatting tests versus true behavioral tests
- identify ownership / expected home for missing tests so the backlog is implementable
- produce a prioritized gap ledger that distinguishes critical-path gaps from cosmetic gaps

## Acceptance Criteria
- future beads can point back to this issue as the canonical inventory rather than rediscovering the current state
- the audit explicitly calls out present realism exceptions such as stub encoders, stub embedders, stub LLM completions, mock installer artifacts, and fake filesystem/repo state tests
- the audit is specific enough to drive per-subsystem implementation work without hand-wavy “add more tests” follow-ups

## Important Notes
This is not a paper exercise. The output must be actionable enough that later beads can close gaps mechanically.","status":"tombstone","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.997462159Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649697626Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649697626Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649644266Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"task","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["coverage","e2e","repo-wide","testing","unit-tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-aazao.1","depends_on_id":"br-aazao","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.997462159Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-aazao.9.2","title":"E2E: comprehensive HTTP transport test covering all 36 tools and 33 resources","description":"The current test_http.sh has 47 assertions. Expand to cover:
- All 36 tools via HTTP POST /mcp/
- All 33 resources via resources/read
- Bearer auth: with token → 200, without → 401, wrong token → 403
- Content-Type validation: non-JSON → 415

## What to build
Add a `Ci` variant to the CLI enum in lib.rs and handle it by orchestrating the
gate runner and report generator.

## CLI interface
```
am ci [--quick] [--report <path>] [--json]
```

Flags:
- --quick: Skip gates marked skip_in_quick (E2E suites). Always produces decision=no-go.
- --report <path>: Write gate report to this path (default: tests/artifacts/ci/gate_report.json)
- --json: Machine-readable JSON output to stdout (summary only, full report goes to --report)

## Behavior
1. Detect project root (git rev-parse or CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR)
2. Set up environment variables (CARGO_TARGET_DIR, DATABASE_URL, etc.)
3. Create output directories (mkdir -p for report path)
4. Run all gates via run_gates()
5. Generate report via generate_report()
6. Print summary to stderr:
   ```
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
     CI Summary: Pass=N  Fail=N  Skip=N
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
     Gate report: <path>
     RESULT: PASSED | FAILED
   ```
7. Exit code: 0 if decision=go, 1 if decision=no-go

## Implementation notes
- Add `Ci(CiArgs)` to the Cli enum in crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs
- CiArgs struct with clap derives for --quick, --report, --json
- The 13 default gates should be hardcoded as a constant (matching ci.sh lines 143-171)
- Per-gate output should be streamed to stderr in real-time (not just at the end),
  so the user sees progress. Use Command::spawn() + pipe rather than Command::output()
  for real-time streaming.

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs (Cli enum addition)
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/ci.rs (run_ci_command function)

 {"id":"br-v3hid","title":"D.2: Wire into TUI render loop replacing current strategy","description":"**Background**

The `BayesianDiffStrategy` from D.1 must be integrated into the actual TUI render loop in the server crate. The current render path in the FrankenTUI integration uses `ftui-runtime` for frame scheduling and `ftui::Frame` for buffer management.

**Scope / Adoption wedge**

1. Add a `BayesianDiffStrategy` instance to the TUI application state struct.
2. At the start of each frame render:
   a. Compute `FrameState` from the current frame metrics (change count from buffer diff, resize flag from terminal events, budget from `AnimationBudget`).
   b. Call `strategy.observe(&frame_state)` to get the `DiffAction`.
   c. Based on the action:
      - `Incremental`: render only dirty regions (use ftui's differential rendering).
      - `Full`: force full redraw (`frame.force_redraw()`).
      - `Deferred`: skip rendering, schedule next frame sooner.
3. Record the decision to the evidence ledger with the FrameState as evidence.
4. After rendering, backfill the outcome: was the frame completed within budget? Did the incremental diff miss any changes (detected by occasional full-diff audit frames)?

**Risks / Safe Mode**

- Risk: Incorrect integration causes visual glitches. Mitigation: Full diff is always correct; the only risk is choosing incremental when full is needed.
- Fallback trigger: If visual glitch reports exceed 0 in testing, switch to deterministic_fallback mode.
- Safe mode: Start with `deterministic_fallback = true` and progressively enable Bayesian mode.

**Validation**

- Render 1000 frames with synthetic data mutations. Verify:
  1. No visual glitches (compare Bayesian render output to full-diff render output).
  2. Frame budget adherence >= 95% of frames complete within 16ms.
  3. Bayesian mode skips fewer frames than a naive "always full diff" strategy.

**Tests (4 required)**

1. `tui_bayes_integration_no_glitch` -- render 100 frames with mutations, compare to full-diff baseline
2. `tui_bayes_resize_triggers_full` -- simulate resize event, verify full diff chosen
3. `tui_bayes_stable_uses_incremental` -- 10 stable frames, verify incremental chosen for most
4. `tui_bayes_evidence_recorded` -- verify evidence ledger has entries after rendering","design":"**Logging requirements:**
- `info!("tui_bayes: integration active, fallback={}", deterministic_fallback)` on startup
- `debug!("tui_bayes: frame={} action={:?} change_ratio={:.2} budget_ms={:.1}", frame_num, action, state.change_ratio, state.budget_remaining_ms)` per frame
- `warn!("tui_bayes: audit mismatch at frame={}, cells_differ={}", frame_num, diff_count)` on audit frame mismatch
- `trace!("tui_bayes: evidence recorded seq={}", seq)` per evidence ledger write","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- BayesianDiffStrategy is integrated into render loop and governs incremental/full/deferred paths
- FrameState is computed from real frame metrics including resize/load/backpressure signals
- Unit tests validate path selection glue logic and fallback switching in render orchestrator
- Integration tests compare rendered output parity across strategy modes on deterministic traces
- E2E PTY test exercises heavy event streams and resize churn with no user-visible regressions
- Evidence ledger records per-frame decision and outcome backfill without dropping records silently
- Diagnostics include frame budget utilization, chosen diff path, and per-frame strategy latency","notes":"## Plan-Space Review Findings (2026-02-13)

**CRITICAL: Only 4 tests for the most complex integration bead.** This bead has the highest risk of visual regressions and needs minimum 8 tests. Add:
5. `tui_bayes_startup_behavior` — first frame with no prior observations uses safe default (full diff)
6. `tui_bayes_rapid_resize_storm` — 10 resize events in 1 second, verify no crash/glitch
7. `tui_bayes_budget_enforcement` — frame with deferred action skips rendering and schedules sooner
8. `tui_bayes_deterministic_fallback_toggle` — transition from fallback=true to false works cleanly

**FIX: Specify integration points.** The bead says "add to TUI application state struct" but doesn't identify WHERE. The struct is `MailAppModel` in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/tui_app.rs`. The view method where rendering happens is `fn view(&self, frame: &mut Frame)` implementing the `Model` trait from `ftui_runtime::program`. The current tick interval is `TICK_MS: u64 = 100` (line 42 of tui_app.rs).

**FIX: Define "deferred" behavior.** When `DiffAction::Deferred` is chosen, what happens? Proposal: skip the current `view()` call entirely, and set the next tick to `TICK_MS / 2` (50ms) to retry sooner. The frame counter still increments.

**FIX: Define startup behavior.** For the very first frame (no prior observations), use `DiffAction::Full` unconditionally. The posterior is uniform, and the loss matrix favors full diff when budget is unknown.

**FIX: Define "audit frame" frequency.** Every 100th frame should be a forced full-diff to detect missed changes. If the full-diff output differs from the cached incremental output, log a warning and bump the posterior toward `Bursty`.

**Performance concern:** Adding `strategy.observe()` + evidence ledger `record()` on every frame at 10fps adds ~2us/frame. Total overhead is 0.002ms, well within budget.

## Refinement Pass 2 Findings (2026-02-14)

**FIX: Audit frame comparison mechanism.** To detect whether incremental diff missed changes, every 100th frame: (1) render the frame using the Bayesian-chosen strategy to buffer A, (2) render the same frame using Full diff to buffer B, (3) compare `buffer_a == buffer_b` cell-by-cell. If they differ, the incremental strategy missed a change. Implementation: use `ftui::Buffer::diff(&self, &other) -> Vec<(u16, u16, Cell)>` if available, or iterate cells. If no `diff` method exists, compare serialized buffer snapshots.

**FIX: "Missed change" response.** When an audit frame detects a mismatch: (1) log at `warn!` level with the differing cell coordinates, (2) call `strategy.observe()` with a synthetic `FrameState { change_ratio: 1.0, ... }` to push the posterior toward Bursty, (3) emit an evidence ledger record with `decision_point: "tui.audit_mismatch"`.  {"id":"br-vjnwp","title":"ATC Track 15: PID Regret Controller — Self-Tuning Loss Matrix via Feedback Control","description":"# Track 15: PID Regret Controller — Self-Tuning Loss Matrix

## Alien-Artifact Source: §33 Control Theory & Feedback Systems
## Key math: PID control, anti-windup, Lyapunov stability

## The Problem This Solves
Track 11's regret tracker identifies WHAT decisions are wrong and by how much.
But it only SUGGESTS loss matrix adjustments that an operator must approve.
Most operators will never tune loss matrix values manually. The ATC needs
to tune ITSELF — safely, with stability guarantees.

## The Control-Theoretic Insight
The loss matrix is a CONTROLLER for the ATC's behavior:
- Higher L(release_reservations, alive) → less aggressive death declarations
- Lower L(advisory_message, no_conflict) → more frequent advisories

Regret is the ERROR SIGNAL: positive regret means the current loss matrix
is suboptimal. A PID controller on regret drives the loss matrix toward
the values that minimize regret — automatically, continuously, safely.

## Why PID, Not FTRL Alone
FTRL (from Track 11) gives optimal gradient updates but can oscillate —
especially when the environment is non-stationary (agents come and go,
workload patterns shift). PID control adds:
- **Proportional term**: immediate response to current regret
- **Integral term**: eliminates steady-state bias (prevents systematic errors)
- **Derivative term**: dampens oscillation (prevents overcorrection)

The PID wraps FTRL's gradient signal: FTRL computes the direction, PID
controls the SPEED and STABILITY of the adaptation.

## Core Implementation

```rust
struct RegretPidController {
    /// One PID per loss matrix entry
    controllers: HashMap<(String, String), PidState>,  // (action, state) → PID
    /// Global adaptation rate (0 = frozen, 1 = fully adaptive)
    adaptation_rate: f64,
    /// Lyapunov stability certificate (tracks if the system is converging)
    lyapunov_v: f64,
    /// Whether auto-tuning is active
    auto_tune_active: bool,
}

struct PidState {
    /// Current loss matrix value
    current_value: f64,
    /// Original (configured) value for reset
    original_value: f64,
    /// PID gains
    kp: f64, ki: f64, kd: f64,
    /// PID state
    integral: f64,
    prev_error: f64,
    /// Anti-windup: clamp integral to prevent unbounded accumulation
    integral_max: f64,
    /// Safety bounds: loss value must stay within [min, max]
    min_value: f64,
    max_value: f64,
}

impl PidState {
    /// Update loss matrix entry based on regret error signal.
    ///
    /// error = recent_average_regret for decisions using this (action, state)
    /// Positive error → loss value is too low (action was chosen too often)
    /// Negative error → loss value is too high (action was avoided too much)
    fn update(&mut self, error: f64, dt: f64) -> f64 {
        let p = self.kp * error;
        self.integral = (self.integral + error * dt)
            .clamp(-self.integral_max, self.integral_max);
        let i = self.ki * self.integral;
        let d = self.kd * (error - self.prev_error) / dt;
        self.prev_error = error;

        let delta = p + i + d;
        self.current_value = (self.current_value + delta)
            .clamp(self.min_value, self.max_value);
        self.current_value
    }

    /// Reset to original configured value (operator override).
    fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.current_value = self.original_value;
        self.integral = 0.0;
        self.prev_error = 0.0;
    }
}
```

## Stability Guarantee (Lyapunov Certificate)
The PID must not destabilize the ATC. We use a discrete Lyapunov function:

```
V(t) = Σ (current_value(t) - optimal_value)²
```

If V is decreasing (V(t+1) < V(t)) averaged over a window, the system is
converging. If V is increasing, the PID gains are too aggressive — halve
them automatically (gain scheduling).

```rust
fn check_lyapunov_stability(&mut self) -> bool {
    let v_current: f64 = self.controllers.values()
        .map(|pid| (pid.current_value - pid.original_value).powi(2))
        .sum();

    let stable = v_current <= self.lyapunov_v * 1.1; // within 10%
    if !stable {
        // Gain scheduling: halve all PID gains
        for pid in self.controllers.values_mut() {
            pid.kp *= 0.5;
            pid.ki *= 0.5;
            pid.kd *= 0.5;
        }
        tracing::warn!("ATC PID gains halved for stability");
    }
    self.lyapunov_v = v_current;
    stable
}
```

## Safety Envelope
- Loss values have hard bounds: each entry stays within [0.1× original, 10× original]
- The PID only runs when AM_ATC_AUTO_TUNE_LOSS_MATRIX=true (off by default)
- When safe mode is active (Track 5), PID is frozen — no adaptation during crisis
- On any Lyapunov instability, PID resets to original values + logs the event
- Operator can reset all values at any time via `am robot atc --reset-loss-matrix`

## What
End-to-end and integration tests that verify the ATC works correctly as a
complete system, not just unit-tested subsystems. This is the final validation
gate before the ATC can be trusted in production.

## Critical Testing Lessons (from this project's history)
1. **ALWAYS use database_url: \"sqlite:///:memory:\"** in tests. Tests using
   Config::default() hit the running server's real DB and become flaky.
   (Learned from br-99dp8 agents test fix, 2026-03-14.)

2. **Theme-related tests need ScopedThemeLock** AND the palette cache must
   detect external theme changes. (Learned from palette cache fix, 2026-03-14.)

3. **Simulated time, not real time.** Tests that sleep() are flaky and slow.
   Pass a virtual clock to the ATC engine instead of using Instant::now().

4. **Deterministic event sequences.** No randomness in scenario tests.
   Property tests use proptest with seed logging for reproducibility.

## Test Categories

### 1. Scenario Tests (in-process integration)
Each scenario creates a TuiSharedState with in-memory DB, constructs an
AtcEngine, feeds scripted events, and asserts on ATC actions.

#### Scenario A: Stale Agent Detection
```rust
#[test]
fn scenario_stale_agent_detection() {
    let state = test_state_with_memory_db();
    let mut engine = AtcEngine::new_for_testing(&state);
    let mut clock = VirtualClock::new();

    // Phase 1: Three agents send messages regularly (60s intervals)
    for tick in 0..30 {
        clock.advance(Duration::from_secs(2));
        if tick % 3 == 0 {
            push_message_event(&state, "AgentA", clock.now_micros());
        }
        if tick % 3 == 1 {
            push_message_event(&state, "AgentB", clock.now_micros());
        }
        if tick % 3 == 2 {
            push_message_event(&state, "AgentC", clock.now_micros());
        }
        engine.tick(&state, &snapshot, clock.now_micros());
    }
    assert_all_alive(&engine, &["AgentA", "AgentB", "AgentC"]);

    // Phase 2: AgentB stops sending messages
    for _ in 0..60 { // 120 seconds of silence from B
        clock.advance(Duration::from_secs(2));
        push_message_event(&state, "AgentA", clock.now_micros());
        push_message_event(&state, "AgentC", clock.now_micros());
        engine.tick(&state, &snapshot, clock.now_micros());
    }

    // Phase 3: Verify ATC sent health probe to B
    assert_probe_sent(&engine, "AgentB");
    assert_status(&engine, "AgentB", AgentLivenessState::Suspect { .. });

    // Phase 4: More silence → Dead declaration
    for _ in 0..120 {
        clock.advance(Duration::from_secs(2));
        engine.tick(&state, &snapshot, clock.now_micros());
    }
    assert_status(&engine, "AgentB", AgentLivenessState::Dead { .. });
    assert_reservations_released(&state, "AgentB");

    // Phase 5: AgentB resurrects
    push_message_event(&state, "AgentB", clock.now_micros());
    engine.tick(&state, &snapshot, clock.now_micros());
    assert_status(&engine, "AgentB", AgentLivenessState::Alive);
}
```

### 2. Property Tests (proptest)
```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn session_summary_monotonicity(events: Vec<SimulatedEvent>) {
        let mut summary = SessionSummary::default();
        for (i, event) in events.iter().enumerate() {
            let before = summary.clone();
            summary.absorb_event(&event.to_mail_event());
            // Every counter must be >= before
            assert!(summary.messages_sent >= before.messages_sent,
                "messages_sent decreased at event {i}");
            assert!(summary.messages_received >= before.messages_received,
                "messages_received decreased at event {i}");
            // Every set must be a superset of before
            assert!(before.active_agents.is_subset(&summary.active_agents),
                "active_agents shrunk at event {i}");
            // Every timestamp must not regress
            assert!(summary.last_event_ts >= before.last_event_ts,
                "last_event_ts regressed at event {i}");
        }
    }

    #[test]

    #[test]

    #[test]
    fn decision_core_picks_min_expected_loss(
        posterior: Vec<(State, f64)>,
        loss_matrix: HashMap<(Action, State), f64>
    ) {
        let core = DecisionCore::new(loss_matrix);
        let (action, loss) = core.choose_action(&posterior);
        // Verify no other action has lower expected loss
        for alt_action in all_actions() {
            let alt_loss = expected_loss(&posterior, &loss_matrix, alt_action);
            assert!(loss <= alt_loss + f64::EPSILON);
        }
    }

    #[test]
    fn tarjan_finds_all_cycles(graph: RandomGraph) {
        let sccs = find_deadlock_cycles(&graph);
        // Every SCC is a true cycle (verify by DFS)
        for scc in &sccs {
            assert!(is_true_cycle(&graph, scc));
        }
        // No cycle is missed (verify by brute-force cycle finder on small graphs)
        if graph.node_count() <= 20 {
            let brute_cycles = brute_force_cycles(&graph);
            assert_eq!(sccs.len(), brute_cycles.len());
        }
    }
}
```
